title: Hyperpigmentation

summary: Hyperpigmentation refers to darkening of the skin due to excess melanin deposition, affecting any skin type but more visible in darker Fitzpatrick phototypes (III–VI), which predominate in India.
The most common causes are post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH), melasma, and drug-induced pigmentation.
Melasma predominantly affects women (90%) and is strongly associated with sun exposure, pregnancy, and oral contraceptive use.
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation follows any inflammatory skin condition — acne, eczema, insect bites, burns — and resolves slowly over months.
Addison's disease and other endocrine causes must be excluded in diffuse or accentuated hyperpigmentation, particularly in flexures.
Dermoscopy, Wood's lamp examination, and biopsy help characterise pigment depth (epidermal vs dermal) and guide prognosis.
First-line treatment is strict photoprotection (broad-spectrum SPF ≥30–50) combined with topical depigmenting agents; systemic treatment rarely needed.
Treatment response is slow (3–6 months minimum); patient counselling on realistic expectations is essential.

qna.question: What is the most common cause of hyperpigmentation in India?
qna.answer: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) secondary to acne vulgaris is the most common cause, followed by melasma.

qna.question: What is the first-line topical agent for hyperpigmentation?
qna.answer: Hydroquinone 2–4% is the gold standard topical depigmenting agent, often combined with tretinoin 0.025–0.05% and a mild topical steroid (Kligman's formula).

qna.question: Which investigations are essential to rule out systemic causes?
qna.answer: For diffuse or flexural hyperpigmentation: serum cortisol (8 AM), ACTH stimulation test, TSH, vitamin B12, LFTs, and blood film for haematological causes.

qna.question: How do you distinguish epidermal from dermal pigmentation?
qna.answer: Wood's lamp accentuates epidermal pigmentation (appears brighter) but not dermal pigmentation. Dermoscopy shows brown colour for epidermal and grey/blue for dermal melanin.

qna.question: What is the most important initial treatment regardless of cause?
qna.answer: Strict broad-spectrum photoprotection with SPF ≥30, reapplied every 2–3 hours on sun-exposed skin. Without this, all other treatments will fail.

qna.question: When should a patient with hyperpigmentation be referred?
qna.answer: Refer if: suspected Addison's disease or systemic cause, failure of 3 months of first-line treatment, suspicion of malignant melanoma, or need for procedural treatment (chemical peels, lasers).

qna.question: What drugs commonly cause hyperpigmentation?
qna.answer: Antimalarials (chloroquine), amiodarone, minocycline, bleomycin, clofazamine, oral contraceptive pills, and heavy metals.

qna.question: How does melasma differ from PIH?
qna.answer: Melasma is symmetrical, involves the centrofacial/malar/mandibular zones, has no preceding inflammation, is hormonally driven, and is chronic and recurrent. PIH follows a specific inflammatory event and is more localised.

definition.text: Hyperpigmentation is a common skin condition characterised by darkening of the skin or nails due to increased melanin synthesis, increased number of melanocytes, or deposition of exogenous pigments. It may be localised or diffuse, acquired or congenital, and can affect the epidermis, dermis, or both.
definition.keyThreshold: Clinically significant when affecting cosmetically sensitive areas or indicating an underlying systemic disorder

riskFactors: Darker skin phototype (Fitzpatrick III–VI) — higher melanocytic activity

pathophysiology.summary: Hyperpigmentation results from dysregulation of melanogenesis — the process by which melanocytes in the basal layer of the epidermis produce melanin via tyrosinase-mediated oxidation of tyrosine to DOPA and subsequently to dopaquinone. UV radiation, inflammation, and hormonal signals (MSH, ACTH, oestrogen) all upregulate tyrosinase activity. In PIH, keratinocyte-derived cytokines (IL-1, TNF-α, prostaglandins) stimulate adjacent melanocytes following skin injury. In melasma, oestrogen and progesterone directly stimulate melanocyte proliferation and melanin synthesis, exacerbated by UV. Dermal melanophages (pigment-laden macrophages) develop when pigment is deposited in the dermis following disruption of the dermo-epidermal junction — this dermal pigmentation is far more resistant to treatment.
keyMechanisms: Upregulation of tyrosinase activity → increased melanin synthesis
Keratinocyte inflammatory signalling → MSH release → melanocyte stimulation (PIH)
ACTH cross-reactivity with MSH receptors → diffuse pigmentation (Addison's)
Dermal melanophage accumulation → treatment-resistant deep pigment
UV-induced reactive oxygen species → direct melanocyte stimulation
pathophysiology.clinicalRelevance: The depth of pigmentation (epidermal vs dermal) directly predicts treatment response. Epidermal pigmentation responds to topical agents in 3–6 months; dermal pigmentation requires prolonged treatment or procedural interventions (lasers, peels). This is the most important practical point in managing any hyperpigmentation.

symptoms.feature: Darkening of skin — may be gradual or follow a trigger event
Preceding inflammatory event (acne, eczema flare, bite)
Generalised fatigue, weight loss, postural dizziness
symptoms.note: Suggests Addison's disease — do not miss

signs.feature: Hyperpigmentation at sites of prior acne or inflammation
Diffuse bronze/grey-brown pigmentation including buccal mucosa, palmar creases, areolae, scars
Velvety hyperpigmented plaques in flexures (axillae, neck, groin)
signs.note: Acanthosis nigricans → screen for insulin resistance / malignancy

signs.feature: Blue-grey pigmentation around mouth/lips + GI polyp history
Fixed drug eruption: oval/round hyperpigmented patches at same site after drug rechallenge
Nail hyperpigmentation (longitudinal melanonychia) + diffuse skin pigmentation

redFlags: Buccal mucosa / palmar crease / scar pigmentation → EXCLUDE ADDISON'S DISEASE (life-threatening if missed)
Rapid onset with systemic symptoms (fatigue, weight loss, hypotension) → Addison's / malignancy
Acanthosis nigricans in non-obese adult → screen for occult malignancy (gastric cancer)
Irregular darkening with asymmetry, border irregularity, colour variation → exclude melanoma
Perioral pigmentation + bowel symptoms → Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
Hyperpigmentation in a child with café-au-lait macules + axillary freckling → neurofibromatosis

severity.criteria: Localised, cosmetically acceptable, no systemic cause, recent onset PIH
severity.management: Photoprotection alone, reassurance, review in 3 months

severity.criteria: Melasma, persistent PIH >3 months, cosmetically distressing
severity.management: Photoprotection + topical depigmenting agent (Kligman's or alternative)

severity.criteria: Diffuse/flexural/mucosal pigmentation suggesting systemic cause, failed 3 months therapy, psychosocial impact
severity.management: Urgent investigation for systemic causes, referral to dermatology/endocrinology

stepByStep.action: Detailed history: onset, distribution, preceding inflammation, drug history (including OCP, antimalarials), sun exposure, pregnancy, family history, systemic symptoms
stepByStep.rationale: Establishes whether PIH, melasma, drug-induced, or systemic cause

stepByStep.action: Full skin examination: distribution pattern, lesion morphology, check buccal mucosa, palmar creases, areolae, scars, nails, and flexures
stepByStep.rationale: Mucosal/palmar/scar pigmentation = red flag for Addison's; flexural = acanthosis nigricans

stepByStep.action: Wood's lamp examination (if available): epidermal pigment accentuated under UV; dermal pigment not accentuated
stepByStep.rationale: Determines pigment depth — critical for treatment planning

stepByStep.action: If systemic cause suspected: FBC, blood film, serum B12/folate, TSH, LFTs, fasting glucose, HbA1c, 8AM cortisol
stepByStep.rationale: Screens for haematological, endocrine, and metabolic causes

stepByStep.action: If 8AM cortisol low or clinical suspicion high: refer urgently for ACTH stimulation test and endocrinology review
stepByStep.rationale: Addison's disease must not be missed — adrenal crisis risk

stepByStep.action: If acanthosis nigricans in non-obese adult: fasting insulin, CEA, endoscopy referral
Skin biopsy: only if diagnosis uncertain, melanoma suspected, or to confirm diagnosis of specific dermatosis
stepByStep.rationale: Histology confirms pigment type, depth, and melanocyte number

keyInvestigations.name: Wood's lamp
keyInvestigations.interpretation: Epidermal: enhanced under Wood's lamp. Dermal: no enhancement.

keyInvestigations.name: 8AM serum cortisol
keyInvestigations.interpretation: <140 nmol/L: likely adrenal insufficiency. >500 nmol/L: excludes. 140–500: requires ACTH stimulation test.

keyInvestigations.name: Serum B12 and folate
keyInvestigations.interpretation: B12 <200 pg/mL: deficiency. Common cause of diffuse pigmentation + nail changes in India.

keyInvestigations.name: Skin biopsy + histopathology
keyInvestigations.purpose: Confirm diagnosis, determine pigment depth, exclude melanoma
keyInvestigations.interpretation: Epidermal melanin vs dermal melanophages; melanocyte count

diagnosticApproach.diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Is there mucosal/palmar/scar pigmentation? → YES: urgent cortisol → exclude Addison's. Step 2: Is there a preceding inflammatory event? → YES: PIH. Step 3: Symmetrical facial pigmentation in woman on OCP/pregnant? → Melasma. Step 4: Drug history positive? → Drug-induced. Step 5: Flexural velvety pigmentation? → Acanthosis nigricans → check glucose/insulin/malignancy. Step 6: Diffuse with systemic symptoms → full metabolic screen.

differentialDiagnosis.distinguishingFeature: Symmetrical centrofacial/malar, women, sun-worsened, no prior inflammation
Follows documented inflammatory event, matches distribution of original lesion
Mucosal, palmar crease, scar pigmentation + systemic features (fatigue, hypotension, weight loss)
Velvety, flexural, associated with insulin resistance or malignancy
Temporal relation to drug, often blue-grey (amiodarone, minocycline), resolves on cessation
Diffuse, nail involvement, megaloblastic anaemia, common in vegetarians in India
Irregular border, multiple colours, asymmetry, rapid change — ABCDE criteria

principles: Treat the underlying cause first — no topical treatment will work if the trigger (inflammation, drug, hormone) is not removed.
Strict photoprotection is the cornerstone of all hyperpigmentation treatment — without it, every other intervention fails.
Set realistic expectations: epidermal pigmentation responds in 3–6 months; dermal pigmentation may take 12–18 months or not respond to topicals.
Avoid skin-lightening products containing mercury or undisclosed steroids — widespread in India, cause irreversible steroid atrophy and mercury toxicity.
Depigmenting agents work by inhibiting tyrosinase or disrupting melanosome transfer — combination therapy is more effective than monotherapy.

byContext.approach: Identify and treat underlying cause. Prescribe strict photoprotection. Counsel on avoiding triggers. Treat active acne/eczema to prevent further PIH. Refer if systemic cause suspected.

drugs.name: Sunscreen SPF 30–50+ broad spectrum
drugs.notes: PA+++ or UVA/UVB coverage. Non-negotiable in all types.

drugs.name: Hydroquinone 2% cream
drugs.notes: Stop for 1 month before restarting. Avoid in pregnancy.

byContext.approach: Above + commence Kligman's formula or modified triple combination. Screen for systemic causes (B12, cortisol, glucose). Wood's lamp if available.

drugs.name: Kligman's formula (Hydroquinone 4% + Tretinoin 0.05% + Fluocinolone acetonide 0.01%)
drugs.notes: Gold standard. Available as fixed combination (e.g. Tri-Luma). Do not use for >4 months continuously.

drugs.name: Azelaic acid 15–20% cream
drugs.notes: Safe in pregnancy. Alternative to hydroquinone.

drugs.name: Niacinamide 4% cream
drugs.notes: Well tolerated, suitable for sensitive skin, available OTC.

byContext.approach: Above + manage systemic causes (B12 supplementation, endocrine referral). Dermoscopy. Chemical peels (glycolic/lactic acid). Referral for laser if no response.

drugs.name: Vitamin B12 (Methylcobalamin) 1500 mcg/day
drugs.notes: If B12 deficiency confirmed. Pigmentation improves with supplementation.

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid 250 mg
drugs.notes: Emerging evidence for melasma. Do not use in thromboembolic risk.

firstLine.note: Mandatory for all types. Reapply every 2–3 hours.

firstLine.intervention: Treat underlying cause (stop causative drug, treat acne/eczema, correct B12 deficiency)

firstLine.note: Gold standard for melasma and PIH. Maximum 4 months continuous.

secondLine.drug: Tranexamic acid (oral 250 mg BD or topical 2–5%)

secondLine.note: Referral to tertiary centre; risk of PIH if not done correctly in dark skin

specialPopulations.modification: Azelaic acid (first-line, FDA category B), Niacinamide. Strict photoprotection essential — melasma peaks in pregnancy.
specialPopulations.caution: AVOID hydroquinone, tretinoin, chemical peels, tranexamic acid, kojic acid. Melasma often improves postpartum.

specialPopulations.modification: Treat underlying inflammatory condition (eczema, insect bites). PIH will typically self-resolve. Photoprotection.
specialPopulations.caution: Avoid hydroquinone <12 years. Investigate café-au-lait macules for neurofibromatosis.

specialPopulations.modification: Photoprotection. Evaluate drug causes carefully (amiodarone common in elderly). Treat B12 deficiency.
specialPopulations.caution: Skin atrophy risk with prolonged topical steroid-containing combinations; shorter courses.

monitoring.action: If no improvement at 3 months, reassess diagnosis, check adherence, consider second-line or refer
Stop steroid component if atrophy develops, continue hydroquinone + tretinoin

monitoring.parameter: Serum cortisol / ACTH stimulation (if Addison's suspected)
monitoring.frequency: Once at diagnosis, then per endocrinology

monitoring.action: Initiate diabetes management if criteria met

whenToRefer: Suspected Addison's disease (mucosal/palmar/scar pigmentation ± systemic features) → URGENT endocrinology
Acanthosis nigricans in non-obese adult → exclude malignancy (gastric cancer)
Lesion suspicious for melanoma (ABCDE criteria) → URGENT dermatology/oncology
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome suspected → gastroenterology for surveillance colonoscopy
Failure of 3–4 months of optimised first-line treatment
Requiring procedural treatment (chemical peels, laser, dermabrasion)
Significant psychosocial distress requiring specialist input
pitfalls: Starting topical treatment without identifying and removing the underlying cause — treatment will fail
Prescribing hydroquinone without mandatory sunscreen — will worsen pigmentation
Continuous use of Kligman's >4 months — causes steroid atrophy, telangiectasia, tachyphylaxis
Missing Addison's disease in diffuse pigmentation — potentially life-threatening
Using fixed-dose steroid + bleaching creams without time limit in dark-skinned patients — common in India, causes severe steroid atrophy
Not warning patients about exogenous ochronosis from prolonged high-dose hydroquinone use
Assuming all facial pigmentation in women is melasma without excluding PIH, drug cause, or systemic disease

pearls: In India, check for Vitamin B12 deficiency in any patient with diffuse hyperpigmentation — vegetarian diet makes this extremely common and easily treatable.
Melasma has a significant hormonal component — always ask about OCP use; switching to a non-hormonal contraceptive may be the most effective single intervention.
Niacinamide 4% is inexpensive, available OTC, safe in pregnancy, and effective — prescribe this while waiting for first-line agents or as adjunct.
The combination sunscreen + tretinoin alone (without hydroquinone) is effective for PIH and avoids the risks of continuous hydroquinone use.

Wood's lamp is cheap, non-invasive, and changes management — invest in one for your clinic. Dermal pigmentation (grey under Wood's lamp) will not respond to 6 months of topical therapy and needs early referral.
Melasma is chronic and relapsing — maintenance therapy with photoprotection and low-potency depigmenting agents is needed even after clearance.
pitfalls: Missing Addison's disease — always check buccal mucosa, palmar creases, and old scars for pigmentation in any patient with hyperpigmentation + fatigue
Prescribing steroid-bleach combinations without a stop date — a widespread problem in Indian dermatology practice leading to steroid-damaged skin
Not warning about the slow timeline of treatment — patients stop treatment at 4 weeks assuming it is ineffective
Treating the pigmentation while ignoring active acne — PIH will keep recurring until acne is controlled
Over-prescribing hydroquinone 4% as monotherapy without tretinoin and a steroid — combination is superior and reduces side effects of each individual component

keyTakeaway: Hyperpigmentation in India is predominantly PIH from acne and melasma — both are manageable with systematic treatment.
Always exclude Addison's disease, B12 deficiency, and drug causes before attributing hyperpigmentation to a purely dermatological cause.
Photoprotection is non-negotiable and is the single most impactful intervention regardless of cause.
Kligman's triple combination (hydroquinone + tretinoin + low-potency steroid) remains the gold standard for melasma and PIH, used for a maximum of 12 weeks continuously.
Treatment response is slow; patient education on realistic timelines (3–6 months minimum) and maintenance therapy is as important as the prescription itself.
